ForbiddenRuleJson.description: Advanced constraint rule. A **forbidden-pair** rule sets `invalid` to exactly two option ids in different groups that may not be selected together. The legacy `[{if, then}]` array shape is rejected with 422 — it saved but never executed.

options.description: at_most_n: the option ids the cap applies to.

requires.description: Condition clauses (AND-folded); the rule applies only when all hold.

type.description: Set to at_most_n for a cardinality rule.

option_scalings.description: Quantity scaling for numbered options: {option_id (string): multiplier}. ADDS to the line's base quantity in proportion to a numbered option's selected amount. Independent of usage_subclauses, which decides *whether* the line is included; option_scalings only affects *how many*. Example: {"8200": 1.5}.
option_scalings.title: Option Scalings

usage_subclauses.description: Conditions that make this line configurable. The subclauses are evaluated left-to-right — each joins the running result with its own operator (AND by default, or OR); the first subclause's operator is ignored. Within a subclause the groupSelections groups are ANDed and the options within a group are ORed. An empty or omitted list means the line is unconditional (always included). This is the same condition grammar constraint rules use in their 'requires' list. See the UsageSubclause schema.
usage_subclauses.title: Usage Subclauses

UsageSubclause.description:
A single condition subclause for a configurable placement / BOM line.

**Boolean semantics** (exactly how the engine evaluates a list of
subclauses — see ``app/utils/conditions.py``):

* The subclauses in the list are combined **left to right**. The first
  subclause seeds the result; each subsequent subclause joins the running
  result using **its own** ``operator`` (``AND`` — the default — or
  ``OR``). There is no operator precedence; it is a pure left fold, and the
  first subclause's ``operator`` is ignored.
* **Within one subclause**, every group listed in ``groupSelections`` must
  have **at least one** of its option ids selected — groups are **ANDed**,
  options within a group are **ORed**. Any ``areaStatuses`` /
  ``areaSubclauses`` conditions are ANDed with the group conditions.
* A placement with **no** subclauses (or subclauses with no predicate) is
  **unconditional** — always included.

**Worked example.** The condition

    Length = 8200mm  AND  Facing = Plastic  AND  (Diameter = Medium OR Large)

is a single subclause with three groups (Length ``11``, Facing ``12``,
Diameter ``13``):

    [{
      "operator": "OR",
      "groupSelections": {"11": [8200], "12": [4501], "13": [7001, 7002]}
    }]

(The ``operator`` on a lone/first subclause is ignored.) To OR two whole
conditions together, use two subclauses and set ``operator: "OR"`` on the
second.

This is the same condition grammar used by constraint rules
(``POST /constraints/rules`` → ``requires``), which additionally accept the
``anyOf`` / ``allOf`` option-id shorthands.

areaStatuses.description: {area_id (string): required_enabled_state (bool)}, ANDed with the group conditions. Use for parts that depend on whether an area is enabled.
areaStatuses.title: Areastatuses

areaSubclauses.description: Advanced: multiple area-status terms combined with their own operators, used instead of a flat areaStatuses.
areaSubclauses.title: Areasubclauses

groupSelections.description: {option_group_id (string): [option_id, ...]}. Within this subclause every listed group must have at least one of its options selected (groups ANDed); options within a group are ORed. Example: {"2860": [6492]}.
groupSelections.title: Groupselections

isStandard.description: Deprecated legacy flag; ignored by the engine (the absence of conditions already means 'always include').
isStandard.title: Isstandard

operator.description: How this subclause joins the running left-to-right result of the preceding subclauses: AND (default) or OR. Ignored on the first subclause in the list.
operator.title: Operator

UsageSubclause.title: UsageSubclause

info.description:
Public REST API for the rattle platform — product configuration, pricing, and quotation.

## Quick Start

1. **Obtain an API key** from the web dashboard under *Settings → API Keys*.
2. **Authenticate** by passing the key as a Bearer token:

```bash
curl -H 'Authorization: Bearer rk_live_abc123...' \
     https://www.rattleapp.de/api/v1/products
```

3. **Explore** this spec interactively using the Swagger UI at `/docs/api/interactive`.

## Authentication

Most endpoints require a **Bearer token** (API key) in the `Authorization` header:

```
Authorization: Bearer rk_live_...
```

API keys are scoped — each key can be granted read/write access to specific resource types:

| Scope | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| `products:read` | Read products, areas, groups, options, languages |
| `products:write` | Create/update/delete products and catalog entities |
| `prices:read` | Read price lists and overrides |
| `prices:write` | Create/update/delete price lists and overrides |
| `customers:read` | Read customers and contacts |
| `customers:write` | Create/update/delete customers |
| `quotes:read` | Read opportunities and quotes |
| `quotes:write` | Create/update/delete opportunities and quotes |
| `configurations:read` | Read configurations and states |
| `configurations:write` | Calculate and finalize configurations |
| `documents:read` | Read document templates, instances, content blocks, directories, structure, and public links |
| `documents:write` | Create/update/delete templates, content blocks, structure, instances; clone, translate, publish |
| `parts:read` | Read parts, BOM, placements, and baselines |
| `parts:write` | Create/update/delete parts and BOM |
| `connectors:read` | Read connectors, endpoints, tasks, and jobs |
| `connectors:write` | Create/update/delete connectors and run tasks |
| `connectors:execute` | Execute connector tasks and fire webhook triggers |
| `webhooks:read` | Read webhook subscriptions and deliveries |
| `webhooks:write` | Create/update/delete webhook subscriptions |
| `webhooks:inbound` | Receive data via inbound webhook endpoints |
| `analytics:read` | Read analytics: pipeline, quotes, option selections, part usage |
| `api-keys:manage` | List, view, create, rotate, and revoke API keys |
| `*` | Wildcard — grants all current and future scopes |

Some administrative endpoints (API key creation, rotation, revocation) require **session authentication** via the web UI.

## Response Format

All responses use a consistent envelope:

- **Single resource:** `{"data": {...}}`
- **Collection:** `{"data": [...], "meta": {"limit": 25, "has_next": true, "next_cursor": "..."}}`

Every resource includes a `links` object with HATEOAS-style `self` and related-resource URLs.

## Money & numbers

The API uses **one representation for money everywhere**: a **decimal string**.

| | |
|---|---|
| **Format** | A plain number in a JSON string: `"12.50"`. `.` is the decimal separator, up to **2 decimal places**. |
| **Never** | No currency symbol, no thousands separators, no scientific notation (`1e3`), no `Infinity`/`NaN`. |
| **On input** | You may send a JSON string **or** a JSON number — `"12.50"` and `12.5` are equivalent; both are normalised to the string form. |
| **On output** | Every monetary field is **always** returned as a string (`"12.50"`), so parse it as a decimal, not a float. |
| **Sign** | Non-negative unless a field's description says otherwise (e.g. a discount preset). |
| **Range** | Option/area/product prices ≤ `99999999.99`; quote, opportunity and part figures ≤ `9999999999.99`. |
| **Currency** | Not sent per field — it is derived from your company's base price list (the `currency` field is read-only). |

Every money field in this spec is typed `string` with `pattern: "^-?\\d+(\\.\\d+)?$"` and an example, so a generated client validates the format for you. Example price payload: `{"base_price": "1499.00"}`.

**Quantities** for numbered options (`option_amounts`, `number_min`/`number_max`/`number_step`) are plain JSON **numbers** and may be **fractional** — e.g. `2.5` metres, `0.5` kg, `1.75` m². Percentages (`discount_percent`, `probability`, …) are plain numbers in the range 0–100.

## Errors

Errors follow [RFC 9457 Problem Details](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc9457) with `Content-Type: application/problem+json`:

```json
{
  "type": "/problems/not-found",
  "title": "Not Found",
  "status": 404,
  "detail": "The requested resource was not found.",
  "instance": "/api/v1/products/999",
  "request_id": "req_a1b2c3d4e5f6g7h8"
}
```

Validation errors (422) include a structured `errors` array:

```json
{
  "type": "/problems/validation-error",
  "title": "Unprocessable Content",
  "status": 422,
  "detail": "The request body contains invalid fields.",
  "errors": [
    {"field": "name", "message": "Field required", "code": "missing"}
  ]
}
```

## Pagination

Collection endpoints use **cursor-based pagination**. Pass `cursor` from `meta.next_cursor` to fetch the next page:

```bash
# First page
GET /api/v1/products?limit=10

# Next page
GET /api/v1/products?limit=10&cursor=eyJhZnRlcl9pZCI6MTB9
```

Default page size is 25 (max 100). When `meta.has_next` is `false`, you've reached the last page.

## Rate Limiting

All responses include rate-limit headers:

| Header | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `X-RateLimit-Limit` | Maximum requests per window |
| `X-RateLimit-Remaining` | Requests remaining in current window |
| `X-RateLimit-Reset` | Unix timestamp when the window resets |
| `Retry-After` | Seconds to wait — sent only on `429` (and `503`) responses |

Rate limits vary by subscription plan. Check the `X-RateLimit-Limit` header to see your current allowance.

### Standard (per-plan) bucket

Most endpoints — including **parts, placements and BOM** (`/parts`, `/parts/{id}/placements`, `/parts/{id}/bom`, `/areas/{id}/placements`, `/parts/export`), products, areas, options, customers and quotes — share a single per-company bucket sized by your plan:

| Plan | Limit |
|------|-------|
| Free | 100/hour |
| Starter | 500/hour |
| Growth | 2,000/hour |
| Scale | 10,000/hour |

The window is one hour and resets on fixed hourly boundaries (see `X-RateLimit-Reset`). A single bulk request (e.g. `GET /areas/{id}/placements` or `GET /parts/export`) is far cheaper against this bucket than fanning out one request per part.

### Per-Category Rate Limits

Some expensive operations have additional rate limits independent of your plan:

| Category | Limit |
|----------|-------|
| Webhook management | 100/hour |
| Document endpoints | 200/hour |
| Configuration compute | 200/hour |
| API key management | 50/hour |
| Connector write operations | 30/minute |
| Webhook event catalog | 60/minute |
| Translation upserts | 30/minute |
| Constraint checks | 30/minute |
| Price override replace | 30/minute |

The **parts, placements and BOM** endpoints (including `/parts/{id}/placements`, `/areas/{id}/placements` and `/placements/batch`) are **not** in this table — they use the standard per-plan bucket above (100/hour on Free), not a separate per-category limit. A single bulk request (e.g. `PUT /areas/{id}/placements` or `GET /areas/{id}/placements`) counts as one against that bucket, which is why bulk beats per-line fan-out.

## Limits & Quotas

| Resource | Limit |
|----------|-------|
| Max request body size | 1 MB |
| Request timeout | 60 seconds |
| Pagination page size | 25 default, 100 max |
| Batch operations per request | 100 |
| Idempotency key TTL | 24 hours |
| API keys per company | 25 |
| API key max lifetime | 365 days |
| Webhook subscriptions per company | 10 |
| Webhook delivery timeout | 10 seconds |
| Webhook delivery retries | 8 (exponential backoff) |
| Webhook delivery history retention | 30 days |
| BOM computation timeout | 30 seconds |

## Optimistic Concurrency

Bulk-replace endpoints for constraints and price lists use version headers (`X-Constraints-Version`, `X-Price-Lists-Version`) for optimistic locking. Read the current version from a GET response, then include it in your write request to detect concurrent modifications.

## Pricing resolution

Several mechanisms can set the price of the same option. `POST /configurations/calculate` resolves them deterministically. **Price resolution** — the *unit price of one option* in one area is chosen by the first rule that matches (highest wins):

1. **Advanced (conditional) price** — when another *currently-selected* option matches the advanced price's `condition_option_id` (for that option/area/price-list). It **replaces** the price below; it never stacks.
2. **Option price-override** — keyed on `(option, area, price_list)`.
3. **area-config price** — the `price` carried by `PUT /options/{id}/area-config`, keyed on `(option, area)` with **no** price list.
4. **`Option.price`** — the base price.

The **line base price** is resolved separately and added to the option prices: it is the product price (or its `ProductPriceOverride` for the price list) plus, for each enabled area, the area price (or its `AreaPriceOverride` for the price list). Quantity scaling (`option_scalings`, numbered-option amounts) is applied *after* the unit price is resolved.

**Pricing presets** (`/products/{id}/pricing-presets`) are stored catalog metadata for the UI; they are **not** applied to the calculated configuration price by this API.

## Webhooks

Subscribe to events (e.g., `quote.created`, `product.updated`) and receive HTTPS POST callbacks. Each delivery includes an HMAC-SHA256 signature in the `X-Webhook-Signature` header, computed from the secret you provide at subscription time:

```python
import hmac, hashlib
signature = hmac.new(
    secret.encode(), request.data, hashlib.sha256
).hexdigest()
assert hmac.compare_digest(signature, request.headers['X-Webhook-Signature'])
```

## Multi-Tenancy

All data is scoped to the company associated with your API key. You can never access or modify another company's resources.

## AI Agent Integration (MCP)

This API is also available as an **MCP (Model Context Protocol)** server for AI agents (Claude, GPT, Cursor, etc.). Use the same API key as a Bearer token.

- **Discovery:** `GET /.well-known/mcp.json`
- **Server endpoint:** `/mcp/` (Streamable HTTP)
- **Transport:** `streamable-http`, `sse`

All REST API scopes apply to MCP tools. See the `x-mcp` extension in this spec for machine-readable details.

put.description:
The bulk **write** counterpart to the GET: send the desired set of placements and the server diffs it against the current state, then creates, updates and (with `prune`, the default) deletes to match. This is the recommended way to import or re-apply a configurable BOM in **one** request instead of one `POST /parts/{id}/placements` per line.

- **Idempotent** — re-sending an identical body is a no-op, so a partially-completed import is safe to retry without duplicating rows.
- **`prune: true`** (default) is a full replace: placements not in the body are deleted, so a revised Stückliste re-applies cleanly and a bad import rolls back cheaply. **`prune: false`** upserts without deleting.
- **Atomic** — on any validation error nothing is written.
- A desired line matches an existing placement on `(part_id, usage_subclauses)`; two lines may share a `part_id` only if their conditions differ. The response returns the full resulting set so the import can be verified without a follow-up read.

Execute multiple operations across different resource types in a single request. Max 100 operations.

**Best-effort, not all-or-nothing.** Each operation is isolated in its own database savepoint, so successful items are committed even when others fail. The response body reports `total`, `succeeded`, `failed` and a per-item `results` array (each with its own status and error), so you can retry only the failed items. HTTP `200` means every item succeeded; `207` means at least one failed. A top-level `4xx` (e.g. malformed envelope or more than 100 operations) means nothing was applied.

Send `X-Idempotency-Key` to make a batch safe to retry: a replay with the same key and body returns the original response instead of re-applying it.

Operation `path`s resolve to top-level collections (`/api/v1/parts`, `/api/v1/parts/{id}`) and, for placements, to both the flat collection (`/api/v1/placements`, `/api/v1/placements/{id}`) and the nested REST shapes (`POST /api/v1/parts/{id}/placements`, `PUT`/`DELETE /api/v1/parts/placements/{id}`). Creating a placement via a flat path requires `part_id` and `area_id` in the body; the nested `/parts/{id}/placements` form takes `part_id` from the URL.

get.description: Combination rules express how options and areas relate beyond simple forbidden pairs. `rule_type` is one of `forced`, `prerequisite`, `warning`, `visibility`, `recommendation`, `default`, or `set_quantity`. Each side (`source`/`target`) is an option or an area (`source_type`/`target_type` = `option` | `area`). Option↔option rules must link different groups, or two options of the same multi-select group. Cross-area rules must stay coherent: a `forced`/`recommendation` rule with an area source requires the target option to be available in that area; `prerequisite`/`visibility` rules reject a gated entity that only exists inside the area it is paired with. `set_quantity` rules couple the target option's quantity to the source via `quantity_factor`, `quantity_offset`, `quantity_rounding` and `quantity_editable`. Requires the `combination_rules` plan feature.

post.description:
Advanced rules use one of two `rule_json` shapes, validated and canonicalised identically to the in-app rule editor:

* **Forbidden pair** — `{"invalid": [a, b]}` with exactly two option ids from different groups (or the same multi-select group). Optional `"direction": "one_way"` plus `"source": a` makes resolution directed (selecting the source auto-removes the target); validity stays symmetric. Omit `direction` for a symmetric rule.
* **N-way mutual exclusion** — `{"type": "at_most_n", "options": [...], "max_selected": N}` with at least two distinct options and `1 <= max_selected < len(options)`.

Both shapes accept an optional `"requires"` list of condition clauses (`anyOf` / `allOf` / `groupSelections`, chained with `"operator": "AND"|"OR"`, default AND) that gates when the rule is active. This is the same condition grammar as a placement's `usage_subclauses` (see the `UsageSubclause` schema) — `groupSelections` and `operator` mean the same thing in both; constraint rules additionally accept the `anyOf`/`allOf` option-id shorthands. All referenced option ids must belong to the product.

post.description:
Execute up to 100 create/update/delete/upsert operations on customers.

**Best-effort, not all-or-nothing.** Each operation is isolated in its own database savepoint, so successful items are committed even when others fail. The response body reports `total`, `succeeded`, `failed` and a per-item `results` array (each with its own status and error), so you can retry only the failed items. HTTP `200` means every item succeeded; `207` means at least one failed. A top-level `4xx` (e.g. malformed envelope or more than 100 operations) means nothing was applied.

Send `X-Idempotency-Key` to make a batch safe to retry: a replay with the same key and body returns the original response instead of re-applying it.

parameters.description: Client-generated key (24-hour TTL). A replay with the same key returns the original response instead of re-applying the operations.
parameters.name: X-Idempotency-Key

post.description:
Execute up to 100 create/update/delete/upsert operations on groups.

**Best-effort, not all-or-nothing.** Each operation is isolated in its own database savepoint, so successful items are committed even when others fail. The response body reports `total`, `succeeded`, `failed` and a per-item `results` array (each with its own status and error), so you can retry only the failed items. HTTP `200` means every item succeeded; `207` means at least one failed. A top-level `4xx` (e.g. malformed envelope or more than 100 operations) means nothing was applied.

Send `X-Idempotency-Key` to make a batch safe to retry: a replay with the same key and body returns the original response instead of re-applying it.

parameters.description: Client-generated key (24-hour TTL). A replay with the same key returns the original response instead of re-applying the operations.
parameters.name: X-Idempotency-Key

post.description:
Execute up to 100 create/update/delete/upsert operations on parts.

**Best-effort, not all-or-nothing.** Each operation is isolated in its own database savepoint, so successful items are committed even when others fail. The response body reports `total`, `succeeded`, `failed` and a per-item `results` array (each with its own status and error), so you can retry only the failed items. HTTP `200` means every item succeeded; `207` means at least one failed. A top-level `4xx` (e.g. malformed envelope or more than 100 operations) means nothing was applied.

Send `X-Idempotency-Key` to make a batch safe to retry: a replay with the same key and body returns the original response instead of re-applying it.

parameters.description: Client-generated key (24-hour TTL). A replay with the same key returns the original response instead of re-applying the operations.
parameters.name: X-Idempotency-Key

put.description:
The bulk **write** counterpart to the GET: send the desired set of direct BOM children and the server diffs it against the current state, then creates, updates and (with `prune`, the default) deletes to match. This imports or re-applies an assembly's bill of materials in **one** request instead of one `POST /parts/{id}/bom` per line.

- **Idempotent** — re-sending an identical body is a no-op, so a partially-completed import is safe to retry without duplicating edges.
- **`prune: true`** (default) is a full replace: children not in the body are deleted, so a revised assembly re-applies cleanly. **`prune: false`** upserts without deleting.
- **Atomic** — on any validation error (unknown child, self-reference, cycle, locked conditions, bad option ref) nothing is written.
- A desired line matches an existing child edge on `child_part_id`; a child part may appear at most once. Conditioned children require the parent to be a ghost assembly. The response returns the full resulting child set so the import can be verified without a follow-up read.

post.description:
Execute up to 100 create/update/delete/upsert operations on placements. `upsert` matches on `(part_id, area_id, usage_subclauses)`, so an import is safe to retry — an identical placement updates the existing row instead of creating a duplicate. For a full declarative replace of an area's placements, prefer `PUT /areas/{id}/placements`.

**Best-effort, not all-or-nothing.** Each operation is isolated in its own database savepoint, so successful items are committed even when others fail. The response body reports `total`, `succeeded`, `failed` and a per-item `results` array (each with its own status and error), so you can retry only the failed items. HTTP `200` means every item succeeded; `207` means at least one failed. A top-level `4xx` (e.g. malformed envelope or more than 100 operations) means nothing was applied.

Send `X-Idempotency-Key` to make a batch safe to retry: a replay with the same key and body returns the original response instead of re-applying it.

parameters.description: Client-generated key (24-hour TTL). A replay with the same key returns the original response instead of re-applying the operations.
parameters.name: X-Idempotency-Key

tags.description: Products are the top-level entities in your catalog. Each product contains areas, groups, and options that define its configurable structure. Scope: `products:read`, `products:write`.
tags.name: Products

tags.description: Areas represent sections or modules within a product configuration (e.g., 'Exterior', 'Interior'). Each area contains option groups. Scope: `products:read`, `products:write`.
tags.name: Areas

tags.description: Upload, replace, and delete images for products, areas, and options. Supports primary images, background images, galleries (up to 10), and area-specific option overrides. All uploads accept `multipart/form-data`. Scope: `products:read`, `products:write`.
tags.name: Images

tags.description: Option groups are containers for related configuration choices within an area (e.g., 'Color', 'Material'). Groups can be duplicated with all their options. Cardinality constraints (`is_required`, `selection_min`, `selection_max`) control how many options must or may be selected. Scope: `products:read`, `products:write`.
tags.name: Groups

tags.description: Options are the individual selectable choices within a group (e.g., 'Red', 'Blue'). Each option has a base price that can be overridden per price-list/area combination. Scope: `products:read`, `products:write`, `prices:read`.
tags.name: Options

tags.description: Price lists represent different pricing contexts (e.g., currencies, regions, customer tiers). Each price list can override option prices. Scope: `prices:read`, `prices:write`.
tags.name: Price Lists

tags.description: Customer records with optional contact persons. Customers are linked to opportunities and quotes. Scope: `customers:read`, `customers:write`.
tags.name: Customers

tags.description: Supported languages for product translations. One language is marked as the base language. Scope: `products:read`, `products:write`.
tags.name: Languages

tags.description: Rules that forbid certain option or area combinations. Includes pair-level forbidden combinations and JSON-based rules for complex logic. Uses optimistic concurrency via `X-Constraints-Version`. Scope: `products:read`, `products:write`.
tags.name: Constraints

tags.description: Technical attributes (number, range, boolean, text) that can be attached to products, areas, or options for specification sheets and filtering. Scope: `products:read`, `products:write`.
tags.name: Attributes

tags.description: The configuration engine resolves option selections against constraints and computes pricing. Configuration states are identified by hash for deduplication. Scope: `configurations:read`, `configurations:write`.
tags.name: Configurations

tags.description: Sales opportunities track deals through stages (qualification → drafting → presented → negotiation → won/lost). Each opportunity has one or more quotes. Scope: `quotes:read`, `quotes:write`.
tags.name: Opportunities

tags.description: Quotes contain line items with pricing. They go through status transitions (draft → approved → presented → accepted/rejected) and support revisions for version control. Scope: `quotes:read`, `quotes:write`.
tags.name: Quotes

tags.description: Full CMS/CCMS document workspace. **Content Blocks** are reusable EditorJS rich-text components organized in directories, with per-language locales and version tracking. **Structure** defines the hierarchical chapter/section tree of a template, with locale titles and content block attachments. **Templates** support deep cloning, inheritance variants (link/extend/fork), and full-template translation via DeepL. **Instances** are rendered snapshots of a template for a specific context (quote, configuration), with publishable output (HTML/PDF/Markdown) and shareable public links. Scope: `documents:read`, `documents:write`.
tags.name: Documents

tags.description: Subscribe to real-time event notifications via HTTPS callbacks. Deliveries include HMAC-SHA256 signatures for verification. Failed deliveries are retried with exponential backoff. Scope: `webhooks:read`, `webhooks:write`.
tags.name: Webhooks

tags.description: Manage API keys for programmatic access. The plaintext key is shown only once at creation — store it securely. Requires session authentication for create/rotate/revoke operations. Scope: `api-keys:manage`.
tags.name: API Keys

tags.description: External system integrations (ERP, CRM, PIM). Connectors define endpoints and tasks that can be triggered manually or on schedule. Jobs track execution history. Scope: `connectors:read`, `connectors:write`.
tags.name: Connectors

tags.description: PLM (Product Lifecycle Management) parts catalog. Parts have placements on areas and Bill of Materials (BOM) hierarchies for manufacturing. Scope: `parts:read`, `parts:write`.
tags.name: Parts

tags.description: Frozen snapshots of a product's part structure at a point in time. Used for engineering change management and audit trails. Scope: `parts:read`, `parts:write`.
tags.name: Baselines

tags.description: Read and update company-level settings (name, default language, URL prefix). Scope: `products:read`, `products:write`.
tags.name: Company

tags.description: Batch/bulk operations for creating, updating, deleting, or upserting multiple entities in a single request. Supports per-item error handling (partial success). Max 100 operations per request. Scope: varies by resource.
tags.name: Batch

tags.description: Revision tracking for parts with lifecycle states (Draft → Review → Released → Obsolete). Only Draft revisions can be edited or deleted. Releasing a revision locks it and obsoletes the previous released revision. Scope: `parts:read`, `parts:write`.
tags.name: Item Revisions

tags.description: Technical document metadata (CAD files, drawings, specifications) associated with parts. Documents can be linked to parts or specific revisions. Scope: `parts:read`, `parts:write`.
tags.name: Part Documents

tags.description: Per-option price overrides scoped to area and price list combinations. Supports individual CRUD and bulk replace operations. Scope: `prices:read`, `prices:write`.
tags.name: Price Overrides

tags.description: Manage catalog filter dimensions and values for the public product selection page. Dimensions group filterable attributes (e.g. Product Line, Material); values are the selectable options within each dimension. Scope: `products:read`, `products:write`.
tags.name: Catalog Filters

tags.description: Receive data from external systems (CRM, ERP). Supports customer upsert, opportunity creation, part import, and connector triggering. Scope: `webhooks:inbound`, `customers:write`, `quotes:write`, `connectors:execute`.
tags.name: Inbound Webhooks

tags.description: Named groups that organize areas within a product. Scope: `products:read`, `products:write`.
tags.name: Area Groups

tags.description: Pre-authenticated configurator links for specific products. Scope: `customers:read`, `customers:write`.
tags.name: Customer Links

tags.description: Product catalog translations and dictionary entries. Scope: `products:read`, `products:write`.
tags.name: Translations

tags.description: Bulk export endpoints streaming NDJSON for incremental sync. Scope: varies by resource.
tags.name: Export

tags.description: Read-only analytics: pipeline snapshots, quote analytics, option selection facts, and part usage facts. Scope: `analytics:read`.
tags.name: Analytics

tags.description: Version control branches for product configuration. Scope: `parts:read`, `parts:write`.
tags.name: Branches

tags.description: Branch review workflows — create, review, approve, and merge pull requests. Scope: `parts:read`, `parts:write`.
tags.name: Pull Requests

tags.description: Engineering Change Requests (ECR) for tracking proposed changes to product configurations. Scope: `parts:read`, `parts:write`.
tags.name: Change Requests

tags.description: Engineering Change Orders (ECO) with impacts and approvals. Track item-level changes and approval workflows. Scope: `parts:read`, `parts:write`.
tags.name: Change Orders

tags.description: Video links and 3D model links for products. Scope: `products:read`, `products:write`.
tags.name: Product Media

tags.description: Read-only safety reference data — GHS/CLP safety logos, H/P (hazard/precautionary) statements, and ANSI Z535 / ISO 3864 signal words used by the `safety_notice` and `hp_statement` document blocks. Scope: `products:read`.
tags.name: Safety
